title: skillc: interactive test tier via orch spawn delegation
description:
Phase 3 from architect design (orch-go-u2y1). Close the intent-vs-action gap.

Currently skillc test uses --print mode (single-turn, text-only). Agents describe what they'd do but never execute tools. The Feb 24 probe proved intent ≠ action (identity-action gap). All existing behavioral measurements are intent-only.

DESIGN (from .kb/investigations/2026-03-04-inv-design-system-prompt-skill-injection.md):

Architecture: skillc owns WHAT to test + HOW to score. orch spawn owns HOW to run agents.

New test mode: skillc test --test-mode full (vs current --test-mode quick which is --print)

Full mode flow:
1. skillc calls orch spawn with scenario prompt as task, skill as system prompt
2. Agent runs interactively with real tool execution (--max-turns to limit)
3. skillc captures session output (--output-format stream-json or transcript)
4. skillc scores tool-call-based indicators

Tool-call indicators (new type):
- calls-orch-spawn: Agent actually invokes orch spawn via Bash
- avoids-task-tool: Agent never calls Task/Agent tool
- uses-read-before-edit: Agent reads files before editing

Sandbox: temp git repo + --dangerously-skip-permissions + --max-turns

Integration contract:
- skillc provides: skill content path, scenario prompt, max-turns, model
- orch spawn provides: agent execution with production-identical injection
- skillc reads: workspace artifacts, session transcript, tool call log

Open questions to resolve during implementation:
- Does --output-format stream-json work in interactive (non --print) mode?
- How does skillc invoke orch spawn and wait for completion?
- Sandbox isolation: temp dir with git init sufficient?

Reference: Architect investigation Finding 7, Phase 3 implementation details.

title: Phase 4: Completion reverse linkage — surface model impact at orch complete
description: Phase 4 of knowledge maintenance automation loop (.kb/decisions/2026-02-15-knowledge-maintenance-automation-loop.md). When orch complete runs, it already checks git diff. Add: 1) Read list of files modified in agent's commits. 2) Cross-reference against all model code_refs (parse code_refs blocks from .kb/models/*.md). 3) If modified files appear in model code_refs, output informational note: 'NOTE: Modified files referenced by models: cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go → .kb/models/completion-verification.md. Consider updating affected models.' This is INFORMATIONAL ONLY — not a gate. Surfaces the linkage at completion review time without blocking workers who legitimately modified code. Acceptance: orch complete shows which models reference modified files when agent changed model-referenced code.

title: Stop hook escape hatch outputs decision:allow but schema expects decision:approve — JSON validation fails on exit
description: When the stop hook fires its escape hatch (Phase: Complete not found after retry), it outputs {decision: 'allow'} but the Claude Code hook schema expects 'approve' or 'block'. The hook error message shows the mismatch. Agent gets stuck in a validation error loop on exit. Fix: change 'allow' to 'approve' in the escape hatch output.

title: Fix harness engineering blog post overclaiming before publication
description:
The harness engineering blog post (blog/src/content/posts/harness-engineering.md, 4,248 words, draft: true) has 6 overclaimed items flagged by claim review probe (2026-03-10). Fix these before publishing:

1. EXPLAIN 265-trial methodology — add section/footnote describing what a contrastive trial is, what was measured, how controls were defined. Currently unverifiable.
2. WEAKEN universality claims — 'any substrate' → 'the substrates we tested'. 'regardless of what substrate' → 'in both substrates we measured'.
3. REMOVE/QUALIFY physics language — 'thermodynamic tendency' → 'tendency'. Define entropy if technical, say it's a metaphor if not.
4. FIX 'better models make it worse' claim — merge experiment shows equal conflict rates. Weaken to 'model improvement doesn't help coordination' or produce directional evidence.
5. QUALIFY 'absent from published literature' — don't claim novelty without systematic review. Weaken to 'underappreciated in the AI coding agent community'.
6. FIX 'language-independent' claim — N=2 (Go + TypeScript) → 'observed in both Go and TypeScript' not 'language-independent'.

Also: move hedging inline (don't bury caveats at end). Blog post should frame around pain ('stop re-investigating solved problems') for Show HN distribution.

Reference: .kb/models/knowledge-physics/probes/2026-03-10-probe-blog-post-uncontaminated-claim-review.md

Phase 3: Session continuity gate — block spawn if prior work unverified
description: Phase 3 of verifiability-first decision. Before spawning Agent N, check if Agent N-1's work has been verified. Block spawn if unverified work exists in the dependency chain. This prevents the cascade pattern where each agent's 'fix' alters ground truth for the next agent without human verification in between. Need: 1) Dependency tracking in spawn context — what prior work does this depend on? 2) Check verification checkpoint file for dependent work. 3) Block spawn with clear message if unverified dependent work exists. 4) Override: orch spawn --bypass-verification 'reason' for independent parallel work. 5) Log bypass to violation log. Note: Independent parallel work (Agent A on X, Agent B on unrelated Y) is explicitly allowed. Only blocks when there's a dependency chain. Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md Phase 3.

description: When bd sync does a pull --rebase and the rebase fails (conflict, autostash issue, etc.), it leaves .git/rebase-merge/ in a broken state. The daemon runs bd sync frequently, so this can silently block all syncing until a human notices.

Add round-robin project fairness to daemon issue selection
description:
## Problem
Daemon sorts ready issues by priority only. When multiple projects have issues at the same priority, whichever project's issues appear first in the merged list monopolizes all slots. This caused PW issues to be starved while orch-go consumed all 3 agent slots.

## Solution
In NextIssueExcluding (pkg/daemon/daemon.go), after sorting by priority, apply round-robin across projects at each priority level. At same priority, alternate between projects rather than draining one project first.

Implementation approach:
1. Group issues by priority
2. Within each priority group, interleave by project (round-robin)
3. Walk the interleaved list for the first spawnable issue

## Acceptance
- At same priority, daemon alternates between projects
- Higher priority still beats lower priority regardless of project
- Single-project case (only one project has work) is unaffected
- Test: 3 orch-go P2 + 2 pw P2 issues → spawns alternate between projects

title: Verification gate inconsistency: spawn gate, daemon preview, and orch review use different definitions of 'unverified'
description:
**Problem:** Three code paths check for unverified work and produce different counts:

1. **orch review** — shows 0 pending completions (correct after clearing backlog)
2. **orch daemon preview** — showed 7 unverified (fixed by orch-go-6fer to filter closed issues in CountUnverifiedCompletions)
3. **orch spawn verification gate** — still shows 6 unverified and blocks spawns, even after the orch-go-6fer fix

The orch-go-6fer fix only patched one code path (daemon's CountUnverifiedCompletions). The spawn gate uses a separate check that wasn't updated. This is an architectural problem: multiple consumers of 'unverified work' state with no shared implementation.

**Impact:** Spawn gate blocks legitimate work. Only escape is --bypass-verification, which undermines the constraint. Observed today: couldn't spawn design session despite all reviewable work being completed.

**Expected:** Single source of truth for 'what work is unverified' that all consumers (spawn gate, daemon preview, orch review) use consistently.

**Evidence:** Session on Feb 16 — completed all items in orch review, spawn still blocked at 6. Had to bypass to spawn design session.

**Related:** orch-go-6fer (first instance fix, daemon preview only). Needs architect to review all verification state consumers and unify.

Write bifurcation post: The Compliance Cliff — Why Agent Frameworks Are Solving the Wrong Problem
description:
Thesis: Most AI agent tooling is compliance tooling (getting agents to follow rules). As models improve, compliance value approaches zero. Coordination tooling (deciding what work to do, in what combination, with what relationship) gets more valuable. We have a working implementation demonstrating the split.

Structure:
- The problem: agent frameworks are 80% compliance, 20% coordination
- The insight: these have opposite value trajectories as models improve
- The evidence: we built a daemon that bifurcates them, with a compliance dial that auto-adjusts based on measured success rates
- The mechanism: Learning Store + ComplianceConfig + Work Graph + OODA loop
- The implication: the system that matters post-compliance is a cognitive resource allocator

Concrete artifacts to reference: 7-commit implementation (Phases 0-4), compliance dial (4 levels), learning store (events → metrics), work graph (dedup/removal), OODA refactor.

Follows harness post (live). Stronger thesis — contrarian, timely, has receipts. Gate effectiveness data (orch-go-00r9c) in 2-4 weeks could be added as an update or sequel.

Prior thread: .kb/threads/2026-03-13-compliance-coordination-bifurcation-designing-split.md

title: Sync spawn mode docs: Claude backend is now primary path (not escape hatch)
Design: grammar-first skill architecture (which 4 behavioral constraints get document slots)
description:
Design work, not implementation.

## Updated framing (post-wou2 experiment, Mar 4)

The original question was 'which 4 behavioral constraints get document slots?' The fabrication experiment (wou2) showed this is the wrong framing. The U-curve reveals that behavioral constraints without organizational knowledge context are WORSE than no constraints — agents over-apply process when they lack calibration context.

**The real design question:** For each behavioral constraint, what is the minimum organizational knowledge needed so it doesn't over-apply?

**Budget (from behavioral-grammars model):**
- ~4 behavioral constraints (the rules)
- ~50 knowledge constraints (organizational context that calibrates the rules)
- Unlimited hard enforcement via hooks
- Unlimited coaching via hooks
- Unlimited reference loaded on demand

**Behavioral constraints are not independent of knowledge constraints.** A behavioral rule without its calibration context creates the U-shaped danger zone (5C: 20% correct on urgency vs bare 100%). The 4 behavioral slots must be designed as matched pairs: rule + calibration context.

**Candidate slots (updated):**
1. Delegation gate + calibration: when delegation applies, when it doesn't (urgency, trivial tasks)
2. Undefined behavior handler: 'when nothing fits, say so' (prevents process over-application at boundaries)
3. Orientation frame + calibration: reconnect Dylan to context, but scale with work weight
4. Pressure resistance + calibration: don't compensate for system failures, but distinguish compensation from pragmatic workarounds

**Key U-curve insight:** Slot 2 (undefined behavior handler) may be the most critical. The 5C agent's failure mode was applying known process to situations that didn't fit. An explicit 'stop and say this is novel' constraint prevents the gravitational pull of nearby-but-wrong process.

**Data:** .kb/investigations/2026-03-04-inv-fabrication-detection-low-constraint-counts.md
**Prior:** behavioral-grammars model (U-curve section), v2 experience, layered enforcement design

title: Add completion_backlog metric detection in Go backend (serve_agents.go)
description:
Phase 6 of coaching metrics redesign (see .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-design-coaching-metrics-redesign.md). Depends on Phase 1 (shared metrics writer).

Detect agents at Phase:Complete for >10 minutes without orch complete being run. Write completion_backlog metric to ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl from Go side.

Implementation: piggyback on /api/agents handler in cmd/orch/serve_agents.go (already polled by dashboard every 30s):
- When serving agent list, check for agents where Phase == 'Complete' and completedAt + 10min < now
- Write metric using shared pkg/coaching writer
- Include session_id and agent beads_id in metric details

This is the first metric written by Go backend rather than TypeScript plugin. JSONL is append-only and safe for concurrent writers.

Note from design: 'breaks the plugin is the only metric writer pattern' — but the alternative (plugin polling agent status) creates circular dependency. Go backend has the data, so Go writes the metric.

title: Architect: Design living threads system for mid-session comprehension capture
description:
Context: Conversation with Dylan revealed a fundamental mismatch between how comprehension works and how the debrief system captures it.

THE PROBLEM:
- Comprehension is dialogic and continuous — it emerges throughout conversation, not at session boundaries
- The debrief is a terminal batch act — asks Dylan to re-articulate what he already said via --learned at session close
- Auto-populated "What We Learned" contains agent explain-back strings (factual summaries), not Dylan's actual insight
- The knowledge placement table has no home for "forming threads" — thinking that's too important to lose but too young to formalize

THE METAPHOR (Dylan's):
- The orchestrator skill is a glove, Dylan's behavior is the hand
- The hand's shape is continuous conversational comprehension
- The debrief glove is shaped for terminal batch processing — it fights the hand

DESIGN SCOPE:
1. Thread artifact type — .kb/threads/ as home for multi-session evolving thinking. Not investigations (point-in-time), not models (mature). Threads carry open questions and accumulate dated entries.

2. Mid-session capture mechanism — How does the orchestrator capture insight as it crystallizes in conversation? Options: new `orch thread` command, incremental --learned that appends, or orchestrator writes directly to thread files.

3. Debrief integration — orch debrief becomes finalization (events + thread pointers), not creation. "What We Learned" references active threads rather than trying to contain all comprehension.

4. Orient integration — orch orient surfaces active threads alongside last session insight.

5. Thread lifecycle — How threads mature into models, decisions, principles. When to promote vs keep evolving.

TOUCHES:
- pkg/debrief/ (debrief generation and quality)
- pkg/orient/ (orient output and debrief parsing)
- cmd/orch/ (new command or flag additions)
- skills/src/meta/orchestrator/ (skill text teaching thread capture)
- .kb/ directory conventions

KEY DESIGN QUESTIONS:
- New command (`orch thread`) vs extending debrief (`--append-learned`)?
- Thread file format — pure narrative? Dated entries? Structured frontmatter?
- How does orch debrief know about active threads to reference them?
- Should threads have explicit lifecycle states (forming/active/resolved)?
- How does this relate to the existing synthesis-as-comprehension plan (extends it? supersedes it?)

SUBSTRATE:
- .kb/plans/2026-03-05-synthesis-as-comprehension.md (the plan this extends)
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-05-inv-design-orchestrator-synthesis-comprehension.md
- behavioral-grammars model (Claim 3: infrastructure > instruction)
- This session's conversation about glove/hand mismatch

Architect: centralized agent lifecycle state machine in pkg/agent/lifecycle.go
description: Agent lifecycle cleanup has 4 independent gaps discovered in one session: (1) orch abandon doesn't remove orch:agent label or assignee → ghost agents in dashboard, (2) orch clean doesn't check process liveness → kills active agents, (3) daemon status reads stale files → false running/paused indicators, (4) daemon sets in_progress but has no orphan recovery → issues stuck forever. Root cause: no single authority owns lifecycle transitions. Design a centralized lifecycle package (pkg/agent/lifecycle.go) that owns every state transition. All commands (spawn, complete, abandon, clean, status) route through it. State machine: spawn→active→completing→closed, spawn→active→abandoned→open. Each transition function handles ALL steps (beads status, labels, assignee, tmux window, workspace, OpenCode session, events). Clean becomes a GC for leaked resources only. Related issues: orch-go-15a2 (clean liveness), orch-go-4omh (stale daemon status), orch-go-8tpn (orphan recovery).

CROSS_REPO_ISSUE:
  repo: ~/Documents/personal/harness
  title: "harness init should not create .claude/ in subdirectories of existing git repos"
  type: bug
  priority: 2
  description: "When harness init runs inside a subdirectory of an existing git repo (e.g. .harness/openscad/), it creates a .claude/hooks/ directory there. This makes Claude Code treat the subdirectory as a separate project root, breaking hook cwd resolution for all agents working in that directory. harness init should detect existing git repos and either refuse or place hooks at the git root."

description:
Phase 1 of verifiability-first mechanical enforcement. The checkpoint file tracks which deliverables have been human-verified through both gates.

Schema per line: {beads_id, deliverable, gate1_complete (comprehension), gate2_complete (behavioral), timestamp, explain_text}

Integration points:
1. orch complete --explain 'text' writes checkpoint entry with gate1_complete=true
2. orch complete reads checkpoints — missing checkpoint for Tier 1 work = cannot close
3. Behavioral gate (gate2) tracked separately — feature/bug requires both, investigation requires only gate1

Currently: ~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl does not exist (ls returns 'No such file or directory').

The --explain flag already exists on orch complete (added by orch-go-w50) but does NOT write to a checkpoint file. It only stores as beads comment. Need the persistent checkpoint for daemon to read.

title: Architect owns decomposition: create plan + beads issues + integration issue for multi-component designs
description:
## Problem

Three-way ambiguity in who creates implementation issues from architect output (61msu). Architect, orchestrator, or plan creation — nobody owns it clearly. Results in component-scoped issues without integration coverage.

## Design

1. Architect Phase 5 gets new step: if recommendations involve 3+ components or multiple phases, architect creates plan artifact (`orch plan create`) AND beads issues for each component AND an integration verification issue that depends on all components.

2. Single-component architect work skips plans — creates implementation issue directly.

3. Integration issue is scoped as behavior verification ("all competitor HTTP goes through proxy"), not component verification ("middleware class exists").

4. Orchestrator Architect Handoff gate tightens: verify plan exists (if multi-component), issues exist, integration coverage present.

## Changes needed

- Architect SKILL.md Phase 5: add 5e (decomposition + plan creation + integration issue)
- Orchestrator SKILL.md Architect Handoff gate: verify integration coverage
- Plan template: add integration issue as standard element

## Evidence

pw-9knh: 6 agents completed, zero integration. All building blocks present, nothing wired together. See orch-go-61msu.

Measure daemon closure rate — what percentage of daemon-produced completions end in a kb artifact or correctly closed loop? Query events.jsonl: count completions with kb quick events vs completions that just got daemon:ready-review and closed. Produce a single number + breakdown by work type.
Daemon verification pause counts discovered work as unverified completions
description: The daemon verification pause threshold treats all triage:review issues equally. But there are two categories: (1) completed agent work awaiting verification, and (2) discovered work awaiting orchestrator review before spawning. Category 2 should not count toward the pause threshold. IMPORTANT: The fix is in the daemon pause counting logic, NOT in the label. triage:review on discovered work is correct — the orchestrator must review discovered work before it gets spawned. The prior attempt (120910fb0, reverted) wrongly changed the label to triage:ready, bypassing orchestrator review. The correct fix: distinguish completed-agent-work from discovered-work when counting toward the verification pause threshold.

Design daemon-agreements integration: auto-create issues from failed agreement checks
description: Close the detection-to-correction loop. Currently kb agreements check surfaces warnings but requires manual intervention. The daemon should poll agreements and auto-create issues when checks fail, so the fix flows through the normal daemon→agent pipeline without human intervention. Design questions: poll frequency, dedup (don't create duplicate issues for same failure), severity gating (only auto-create for error, not warning?), cross-project agreements.

title: Formalize spawn transition in pkg/agent/lifecycle.go — migrate from pkg/spawn/atomic.go

Design autonomous cognitive loop — daemon detects patterns (orphan clusters, hotspot acceleration, thread staleness, model contradictions), triggers investigations, synthesizes into models/articles, surfaces decisions to Dylan. Three layers: autonomous trigger, synthesis-as-product, decision protocol (autonomous/propose-and-act/genuine decision). Must address creation/removal asymmetry. Extends bifurcation architecture.
description: Dylan wants the system to think autonomously and surface conclusions rather than wait for instructions. The daemon already has poll-spawn-complete. Missing: upstream pattern detection that triggers investigation, and downstream packaging of thinking into consumable artifacts. The bifurcation (compliance/coordination split) just shipped — this is the next evolution of the coordination layer. Key constraint: autonomous creation without autonomous removal leads to accretion, so the system must retire its own work too.

title: Fix daemon completion processing to surface errors instead of silent continue
description: In cmd/orch/daemon.go:425-427, when CompletionOnce fails, the code logs an error but continues the daemon loop. This violates the spawn prerequisite fail-fast constraint (kb-035b64). Risk: Completed agents never get marked ready-for-review, orphaned work accumulates, verification pause never triggers. Fix: Surface completion processing errors in daemon health status, count consecutive failures, pause spawning if completion processing is persistently broken (suggests beads database issues).

Add accretion metric to orch complete: track file growth/shrinkage per agent
description:
## Goal

During orch complete, analyze the agent's git diff and log which files grew vs shrank. Over time this tells us whether accretion gravity is inverting.

## Metric to capture

For each file in the diff:
- file path
- lines added / lines removed
- net delta
- whether file is >800 lines (accretion risk zone)

Log to ~/.orch/events.jsonl as an accretion.delta event type.

## Future use

- Dashboard can show "lines added to files >800 lines" trend over time
- Alerts when monolithic files are growing despite extraction efforts
- Proves or disproves that strangler fig strategy is working

## Priority

P2 — not urgent, but the sooner we have data the sooner we can measure. Create after probe confirms the overall strategy works.

title: orch complete verification fails for cross-repo spawns — only checks current repo for commits
description:
When verifying agent work via orch complete, the orchestrator and verification pipeline only check the current repo git log. Agents that work in a different repo (e.g., skillc) commit there, not in orch-go. Verification finds no commits and concludes fabrication.

Fix: Parse AGENT_MANIFEST.json project_dir or beads comments for actual working directory. Check commits in that repo, not just the current one. SPAWN_CONTEXT.md already has PROJECT_DIR — verification should use it.

Impact: False abandonment of completed work, wasted investigation spawn, wasted re-spawn cycles. This session lost ~30 minutes on a non-problem.

description:
Auto-created from architect review orch-go-zrrb.

## Architect Summary
Designed `orch hook test` (simulate hook invocations outside sessions), `HOOK_TRACE` (runtime visibility), and `orch hook validate` (config linting) to eliminate the four hook debugging pain points: invisible env vars, wrong output format with no feedback, silent success, and requiring new sessions per iteration.

## Architect Next Section
**Recommendation:** spawn-follow-up

### Implementation Issues to Create

**Issue 1: `orch hook test` CLI command**
- **Skill:** feature-impl
- **Priority:** P2 (medium — immediate pain reduction)
- **Context:** Implement `orch hook test` command in cmd/orch/ that reads settings.json, resolves matchers, constructs JSON input, runs hooks, and validates output format. See investigation for full CLI spec.
- **File targets:** `cmd/orch/hook_cmd.go` (new), `pkg/hooks/resolver.go` (new — settings.json parser + matcher resolution), `pkg/hooks/schema.go` (new — output format validation)

**Issue 2: `HOOK_TRACE` runtime tracing**
- **Skill:** feature-impl
- **Priority:** P3 (backlog — useful but less urgent)
- **Context:** Add `hook_trace.py` shared module to `~/.orch/hooks/`, add trace calls to existing hooks, implement `orch hook trace` viewer command.

**Issue 3: `orch hook validate` config linter**
- **Skill:** feature-impl
- **Priority:** P3 (backlog — catch config errors)
- **Context:** Implement `orch hook validate` command that checks settings.json hook configuration for common errors (missing files, bad matchers, missing timeouts).

title: orch settings command to modify ~/.claude/settings.json from agents
description:
Agents can't modify ~/.claude/settings.json due to sandbox EPERM. They leave manual instructions that Dylan forgets, leading to silently incomplete work. Since orch CLI runs unsandboxed, add an orch command (e.g. orch settings add-hook <type> <command>) that modifies settings.json directly. Agents call this instead of leaving manual steps.

Common operations needed:
- Add a hook to SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop arrays
- Remove a hook
- List current hooks

This eliminates the last-mile friction where agent work is 'complete' but requires a manual change that never happens.

title: Epic: Redesign work-graph dashboard to surface verification state, group dep chains, and prioritize in-progress work
description:
## Goal

Redesign the work-graph (Issues view) in the SvelteKit dashboard to solve 6 identified problems:
1. Related issues scattered - dep chains not grouped as clusters
2. In-progress items buried - not surfaced to top
3. Decision/issue hierarchy inverted
4. Sort logic unclear and single-dimensional
5. Verification gates invisible - daemon pause state only visible in terminal
6. Verification status not visible on individual issues

## Scope

**In scope:**
- Build missing /api/beads/graph endpoint (prerequisite for all other work)
- Wire attention store to /api/attention (currently a stub)
- Add status-first partitioning (in_progress at top)
- Surface verification tracker state in daemon status bar
- Show verification badges on individual issues
- Add dep-chain grouping mode

**Out of scope:**
- Knowledge-tree page (separate from work-graph)
- Timeline view changes
- Full graph visualization (D3/force-directed)
- Decision/issue hierarchy inversion (complex, needs separate investigation)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] /api/beads/graph endpoint returns nodes+edges for open issues
- [ ] In-progress issues always appear at top of Issues view
- [ ] Verification gate status visible in daemon bar when paused
- [ ] Per-issue verification badges visible (verify, verify_failed, unverified)
- [ ] Dep-chain grouping clusters connected issues together

## References
- Issue: orch-go-r3zy (design session)
- Decision: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
- Probe: .kb/models/dashboard-architecture/probes/2026-02-15-knowledge-tree-sse-cycling-fix.md

title: Audit: 4 verifiability-first issues closed without actual verification
description:
Four P1 issues from the verifiability-first decision were closed by agents that claimed Phase: Complete, but the work either didn't deploy or wasn't wired:

1. orch-go-6th (skill update): Agent updated source SKILL.md but never ran skillc deploy. Deployed skill has none of the changes. → New issue: orch-go-0psz
2. orch-go-7jl (daemon pause): Agent created VerificationTracker with passing tests but never wired it into daemon.go run loop. Dead code. → New issue: orch-go-ydzu  
3. orch-go-tyi (explain-back gate): Agent added the gate but it was later reworked by orch-go-w50. Need to verify the reworked version actually gates.
4. orch-go-w50 (rework explain-back): Agent claims --explain flag works. Flag exists but doesn't write to checkpoint file, just beads comment.

ROOT CAUSE: These were all completed by the previous orchestrator session which was itself violating verifiability-first (batch-completing without Gate 1 or Gate 2). The irony: the issues that implement verification were themselves never verified.

This is the pattern the decision warned about: 'Documentation without enforcement = data-plane artifact agents can ignore.' The agents produced locally-correct code (tests pass, build succeeds) that doesn't compose into a working system.

LESSON: Verifiability-first implementation issues must themselves be Tier 1 verified. Can't skip verification on the verification system.

Wire attention store to /api/attention endpoint (replace stub)
description:
## Context

The attention store (web/src/lib/stores/attention.ts) is a stub - fetch() is a no-op that logs a warning. The backend /api/attention endpoint exists and returns rich attention signals (verify, stuck, unblocked, verify_failed, etc.) but the frontend doesn't consume it.

The work-graph page expects attention to provide:
- completedIssues: CompletedIssue[] (for completed tab)
- signals: Map<issueId, {badge, reason}> (for per-issue badges)

## Task

Replace the stub attention store with a real implementation:
1. Fetch from GET /api/attention?project={projectDir}
2. Transform AttentionAPIResponse.items into:
   - completedIssues (filter signal=recently-closed)
   - signals Map (map subject -> {badge: AttentionBadgeType, reason: summary})
3. Map attention signal types to badge types:
   - recently-closed + unverified -> 'verify'
   - recently-closed + verified -> 'recently_closed'
   - recently-closed + needs_fix -> 'verify_failed'
   - likely-done -> 'likely_done'
   - stuck -> 'stuck'
   - unblocked -> 'unblocked'
4. Preserve existing formatRelativeTime and ATTENTION_BADGE_CONFIG exports
5. Update ATTENTION_BADGE_CONFIG to include all badge types with colors

## Files to modify
- web/src/lib/stores/attention.ts - replace stub with real implementation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] attention.fetch(projectDir) calls /api/attention and populates store
- [ ] $attention.completedIssues populated from recently-closed signals
- [ ] $attention.signals Map populated from all signal types
- [ ] ATTENTION_BADGE_CONFIG has config for all AttentionBadgeType values
- [ ] npm run build succeeds

Fix dashboard knowledge tree SSE cycling: disconnected state causes full re-render loop
description:
The /knowledge-tree dashboard page shows "disconnected" (red dot) for SSE and cycles through different tree configurations, resetting scroll position and expand/collapse state on each re-render.

SYMPTOMS:
1. Red dot "disconnected" in top right — SSE connection not establishing or repeatedly dropping
2. Tree visually cycles through different expand/collapse states
3. Scroll position resets on each cycle
4. User cannot browse the tree because it keeps re-rendering

ROOT CAUSE (likely):
SSE client is reconnecting in a loop. Each reconnection (or failed attempt) triggers a full tree re-render from /api/tree, which resets all UI state (expanded nodes, scroll position).

FIX APPROACH:
1. Fix SSE connection — why is it showing "disconnected"? Check /api/tree SSE endpoint, CORS, event format
2. Preserve expand/collapse state across re-renders — store in localStorage or component state, reapply after data update
3. Preserve scroll position across re-renders
4. Debounce SSE updates — dont re-render on every event, batch updates
5. Diff-based updates — only update changed nodes instead of full tree replacement

MINIMUM FIX: Even if SSE stays disconnected, the tree should render ONCE and stay stable. The cycling is the critical bug — SSE can be fixed separately.

VERIFICATION:
- Tree renders once and stays stable (no cycling)
- Expand/collapse state persists across data updates
- Scroll position preserved
- If SSE connects: live updates appear without resetting tree state
- If SSE disconnected: tree shows static snapshot, no cycling

title: Build /api/beads/graph endpoint - return nodes and edges for work-graph
description:
## Context

The work-graph frontend (web/src/lib/stores/work-graph.ts:122) calls GET /api/beads/graph but the endpoint doesn't exist in serve.go. This is the critical missing piece that makes the entire work-graph page non-functional for dependency data.

## Task

Add handleBeadsGraph() to cmd/orch/serve_beads.go that:
1. Lists open issues via beads CLI/RPC (bd list --json --status=open + --status=in_progress)
2. Parses dependencies from each issue to build GraphEdge array
3. Returns WorkGraphResponse{nodes, edges, node_count, edge_count}
4. Uses beadsStatsCache pattern with 15s TTL
5. Supports project_dir, scope, parent query params

## Data shape (from bd list --json)
Each issue has: id, title, status, priority, issue_type, labels, created_at, dependencies[]
Dependencies have: issue_id, depends_on_id, type (blocks)

## Files to modify
- cmd/orch/serve_beads.go - add handleBeadsGraph handler
- cmd/orch/serve.go - register route mux.HandleFunc("/api/beads/graph", ...)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] GET /api/beads/graph returns JSON with nodes[] and edges[]
- [ ] go build ./cmd/orch/ succeeds
- [ ] Nodes include: id, title, type, status, priority, source, labels, created_at
- [ ] Edges include: from, to, type (blocks/parent-child)
- [ ] Cache with 15s TTL prevents repeated bd subprocess spawns

description:
Auto-created from architect review orch-go-nn43.

## Architect Summary
Architect analysis of kb context query derivation found that 4 prior patches to search quality are a Coherence Over Patches signal — the root cause is that scoring ignores keyword coverage. A keyword coverage multiplier in kb-cli's scoring resolves all symptoms at once.

## Architect Next Section
**Recommendation:** spawn-follow-up (implementation in kb-cli)

### Implementation Work

The existing orch-go-amty issue (MinStemmedScore implementation) covers part of this. The keyword coverage multiplier should be added to the same or a follow-up issue.

CROSS_REPO_ISSUE:
  repo: ~/Documents/personal/kb-cli
  title: "Add keyword coverage multiplier to searchFileContentsWithScore scoring"
  type: feature
  priority: 2
  description: "KB context scoring is blind to keyword coverage — 1/5 keywords matching in title scores 10+ (same as 5/5). Add coverage multiplier: score *= matchedKeywords/totalKeywords with floor of 0.3. Affects searchFileContentsWithScore in search.go. See orch-go investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-27-design-fix-kb-context-query-derivation.md"

description:
## Context

In-progress issues are buried in the tree, sorted by priority/ID like everything else. Users must scan the entire tree to find what's actively being worked on.

## Task

Add a status-first partition to the work-graph Issues view:
1. Extract in_progress nodes from the tree into a dedicated section
2. Render 'In Progress' section at the top (below 'Ready to Complete', above main tree)
3. Style similarly to 'Ready to Complete' section (colored border, header with count)
4. Show agent info (phase, runtime, model) inline if available from active_agent field
5. Keep in_progress nodes in the main tree as well but with a visual indicator they're pinned above

## Design

The section appears between 'Ready to Complete' and the main tree:
- Ready to Complete (emerald) - agents done, awaiting review
- In Progress (blue/amber) - actively being worked on
- [main tree] - everything else (open, blocked)

orch clean can't purge ghost registry entries from derived state sources
description: Problem: After agents die (from orch complete Overmind cascade, missing projects, etc), orch status shows zombie entries indefinitely. orch clean handles disk state (OpenCode sessions, workspaces, investigation files) but the 4 derived state sources (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows, beads issues, workspaces) still produce ghost entries. Examples from today: pw-8966 (project not on disk), pw-8975, orch-go-y55 (already completed), untracked-Feb12-2144, untracked-Feb12-2219, untracked-Feb13-0933 — all showing as 'Active: 8' despite zero live agents. orch abandon fails on cross-project and untracked IDs. Solution: orch clean needs a --ghosts or --registry-purge flag that identifies entries with no live tmux window, no live OpenCode session, and no active beads issue, then removes them from the derived state. Or orch status needs to filter out entries that have no live backing source. Evidence: After running orch clean --all today, 8 ghost entries persisted. Zero tmux windows, zero live OpenCode sessions, but orch status still reports Active: 8.

title: orch tree Phase 1b: relationship parsing, decision/model nodes, and work view
description:
Phase 1a (orch-go-3wu) shipped the tree renderer and filesystem-based clustering. But the core value — the relationship graph where decisions hang off investigations and issues hang off decisions — is missing.

WHAT IS MISSING (from Dylan review of Phase 1a):
1. No relationship parsing — investigations are flat lists, not linked via Prior-Work tables or Evidence sections
2. No decisions or models in tree — only investigations from synthesized/ directories appear
3. Work view is empty — orch tree --work renders nothing
4. Uncategorized is a black hole — 800+ investigations not in synthesized/ subdirs dumped there
5. Default should be --depth 2 (full tree is 1,129 lines, unusable)

SCOPE:
1. Parse Prior-Work tables in investigations to build parent-child edges
2. Parse Evidence sections in decisions to link decisions → investigations
3. Parse Synthesized From headers in models to link models → investigations
4. Add .kb/decisions/*.md as ★ decision nodes in the tree
5. Add .kb/models/*/ as ◆ model nodes with probes as children
6. Link beads issues to decisions/investigations via description references
7. Implement orch tree --work (issues grouped by state with knowledge provenance)
8. Change default depth to 2 (--depth flag still overrides)

TARGET OUTPUT (from design doc — this is what "wow" looked like):

orch-go knowledge tree
│

Architect minimal harness for OpenSCAD agent orchestration
description: Design the portable harness template for OpenSCAD based on experiential eval findings (orch-go-5idhz). Deliverables: CLAUDE.md for an OpenSCAD project, spawn gate definitions (assert-based parameter validation, manifold check, slicer printability gate), completion pipeline, skill template for 'design-part' and 'iterate-design' workflows. Include LLM-as-gate design for intent alignment checking. The goal is a harness template that proves the self-measurement methodology transfers to non-code domains. Reference: .kb/investigations/2026-03-16-eval-openscad-headless-agent-workflow.md, .kb/threads/2026-03-16-cross-domain-harness-portability-can.md

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design the minimal harness for OpenSCAD agent orchestration. This is the portable harness template that proves structural enforcement transfers to non-code domains.

CONTEXT — Read these first:
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-16-eval-openscad-headless-agent-workflow.md (experiential eval findings)
- .kb/threads/2026-03-16-cross-domain-harness-portability-can.md (the thread driving this work)
- .kb/models/harness-engineering/model.md (the framework being tested for portability)

KEY FINDINGS FROM EVAL:
- assert() in .scad = spawn gates (exit 1, parseable errors, first-fail)
- echo() = coaching signals (non-blocking advisory)
- --summary-file = structured telemetry (JSON: bounding box, vertex count, render time)
- Manifold backend 232x faster than CGAL (use Manifold for iteration, CGAL for validation)
- BLIND SPOT: zero printability awareness — needs external slicer gate (PrusaSlicer CLI)
- -D flags enable parameterized design space exploration without code changes

DELIVERABLES:
1. CLAUDE.md template for an OpenSCAD project — what constraints, what guides, what the agent needs to know
2. Gate stack design:
   - Layer 1: Parameter validation (assert-based, in .scad files)
   - Layer 2: Geometry validation (manifold check via CGAL pass, vertex/facet bounds)
   - Layer 3: Printability validation (slicer CLI integration — wall thickness, overhang, support requirements)
   - Layer 4: Intent alignment (LLM-as-gate — does this design serve the declared purpose?)
3. Completion pipeline — what gets checked before a design is 'done'
4. Skill templates: 'design-part' (create from spec) and 'iterate-design' (explore parameter space)
5. Measurement plan — how to measure gate precision/recall using the same methodology as the self-measurement report
6. Accretion equivalent — what 'file bloat' means in OpenSCAD (polygon count? boolean depth? module count?) and how to gate it

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- The harness should be MINIMAL — don't over-engineer. Start with what's mechanically gateable and add judgment layers only where needed.
- The LLM-as-gate for intent alignment is the novel contribution. Design it concretely: what are its inputs, outputs, and how do you measure its precision?
- Think about what the self-measurement report would look like for this domain. What numbers would you report? What would you try to falsify?

OUTPUT: Produce recommendations as a structured architect document. Create beads issues for implementation work.

Redesign work tab: grouping, sorting, and information hierarchy
description:
**Problem:** The work tab in knowledge-tree has multiple structural issues that make it hard to use:

1. **Related issues scattered** — dependency chains (e.g. verifiability-first Phase 1→2→3→4→5) are spread across the list instead of grouped. Issues sharing area: labels but without direct deps are also interleaved with unrelated work.

2. **In-progress items buried** — active work appears at arbitrary positions based on current sort, not surfaced to the top where it belongs.

3. **Decision/issue hierarchy inverted** — decisions appear as children of issues, but the real relationship is usually the reverse: decisions produce issues (decision → implementation issues). Current rendering implies issues are primary and decisions are subordinate.

4. **Sort logic unclear and insufficient** — no single dimension (priority, date, status, dependency) produces a useful ordering. Needs multi-dimensional: status urgency first, then grouping by dep chain / area label, then priority within groups.

5. **Verification gates invisible** — the verifiability-first constraint (Phase 3 session continuity gate) blocks spawns when unverified work exists, but this state is only visible when you hit the wall in the terminal. The dashboard should show: what's awaiting verification, what gates are blocking, and what action clears the block. Without this, the human can't understand or manage the system's constraints.

6. **Verification status not visible on issues** — completions awaiting review need to surface with tier and gate status (related: orch-go-aygz Phase 5).

**Desired outcome:** Work tab that groups related work together, surfaces active/blocked/awaiting-review items prominently, correctly represents the relationship between decisions and issues, and makes verification gate state visible so the human can manage the system proactively.

**Evidence:** Screenshot of current state shows verifiability-first phases scattered across 5+ screen positions despite being a single linear dependency chain. Orchestrator hit verification gate blind when trying to spawn — gate state was invisible until spawn failed.

When --reason contains a beads ID like 'fcrf', the hook matches it as an agent-worked issue and blocks the close. title: Add contradiction scenario (09): two agents with conflicting findings
description: Design scenario where two completed agents report contradictory findings about the same area. Throughput response: orch complete both, move on. Comprehension response: notices the contradiction, surfaces it, asks what it means. Works with current contains X|Y|Z detection grammar — throughput and comprehension say observably different things in this situation. Detection: 'response contains contradict|conflict|disagree|inconsistent|but agent' (positive) + 'response does not contain all done|cleared|completed both' (negative). Part of contrastive scenario series testing comprehension vs throughput.

description: Recurring problem: .kb/models/ artifacts describe system behavior but don't get updated when code changes. Examples: agent-lifecycle-state-model.md still referenced registry after pkg/registry/ was deleted (fixed manually during Feb 13 refactor). Models describe code paths, invariants, and constraints that become stale as implementation evolves. The probe system was designed to test model claims, but probes are manual and reactive — they don't catch drift from routine code changes. Need architectural decision on how to keep models accurate. Options might include: (1) code-change hooks that flag potentially-affected models, (2) model-code linkage metadata that enables staleness detection, (3) periodic model validation as part of completion workflow, (4) acceptance that models are approximate and only update during synthesis. Prior art: .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-models-as-understanding-artifacts.md, .kb/models/PHASE4_REVIEW.md, constraint 'D.E.K.N. Next field must be updated when marking Status: Complete'.

Selection is random — not based on priority, area, or any heuristic Dylan could predict. The unpredictability is the design feature: Dylan's verification grammar cannot converge around a pattern it cannot predict. Implementation: cron or launchd job that runs weekly, selects from recently closed issues, creates a review reminder (notification or beads label). Success criteria: Dylan reviews flagged completions at deeper-than-usual depth, occasionally catches something the normal review process missed. Connects to coupling theory: strategic illegibility applied to human verification patterns. This requires tooling that understands spreadsheet semantics (formulas, cell references, charts, tabs). title: Fix spawn template: remove default investigation deliverable from all spawns
description:
## Problem

pkg/spawn/context.go:203-226 tells ALL worker spawns to create investigation files. This is the root cause of the 312:1 investigation-to-synthesis ratio (936 investigations, 3 synthesized).

Only 5-7 skills should produce investigations as primary deliverables: investigation, architect, research, codebase-audit, reliability-testing. Feature-impl only when --phases includes investigation.

## Fix

1. Remove investigation deliverable setup from default spawn template (context.go:203-226)
2. Make investigation production skill-specific — only inject investigation deliverable for skills that should produce them
3. Expected result: ~70% reduction in investigation file creation

## Evidence

- Root cause analysis: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-investigate-skills-produce-investigation-artifacts.md
- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 45 in February alone
- Feature-impl agents for bug fixes creating unnecessary investigations (og-feat-fix-claude-md, og-feat-implement-kb-reflect, etc.)

## Scope

Surgical fix to context.go spawn template. Do NOT refactor the entire spawn system — just gate the investigation deliverable on skill type.

[orch-go] architect: ARCHITECT SUBPROBLEM 1: AUTONOMOUS TRIGGER LAYE...
Daemon treats relates_to links as blocking dependencies
description: When daemon preview evaluates whether an issue is spawnable, it treats relates_to links as blocking dependencies. Evidence: orch-go-nlgg had a relates_to link with orch-go-k793 (informational — 'these are related audits'). Daemon preview showed nlgg as 'blocked by dependencies: orch-go-k793 (open)' and refused to spawn it. Orchestrator had to run bd dep unrelate to unblock. relates_to is informational ('these are related'), not blocking ('this must complete first'). Only blocks edges should prevent spawning. Impact: every relates_to link in the system is potentially preventing issues from being spawned. This may have silently blocked work in previous daemon cycles without anyone noticing — the daemon just skips blocked issues and moves on.

title: Orchestrator HUD: persistent system state display via Claude Code hook
description: Orchestrator forgot daemon was off within the same session — said 'daemon will pick it up' 20 minutes after deciding 'daemon stays off.' Need: 1) orch daemon hold/release commands that write a marker file, 2) daemon run refuses to start while hold exists, 3) SessionStart hook (or equivalent) that surfaces critical system state at the start of every orchestrator response — daemon status, hold reasons, unverified completion count, active agents. The orchestrator should never have to ask 'is the daemon running?' — it should always know.

title: harness init registers project-local hooks at user-level scope with relative paths
description: harness init in toolshed wrote .claude/hooks/gate-git-add-all.py (project-local) but registered it in ~/.claude/settings.json (user-level) with a relative path. This breaks every Bash call in every other project. Two fixes needed: (1) harness init should write to project-level settings when script is project-local, OR copy to ~/.orch/hooks/ with absolute path matching existing convention. (2) harness init should detect existing equivalent hooks (gate-worker-git-add-all.py already existed in ~/.orch/hooks/).

title: Implement cross-repo visibility for kb context and kb reflect — agents spawned with 40% of available knowledge
description: kb context and kb reflect both use findKBDir() which only sees the current project's .kb/. Cross-repo inventory found 3,287 artifacts across 20+ repos but only 1,331 (40.5%) visible. This means: (1) agents are spawned with incomplete knowledge — missing decisions, constraints, and principles from ~/.kb/ and other repos, (2) kb reflect can't detect synthesis opportunities across repos. Architect investigation exists at .kb/investigations/2026-02-26-design-global-store-reflection-path.md recommending virtual project with discoverProjects(). Implementation target: kb-cli repo. Must cover both kb context (spawn-time knowledge injection) and kb reflect (maintenance reflection). The daemon --global fix (orch-go-c1dc) partially addressed reflect but kb context has no --global equivalent yet.

title: Architect completion advisory gate — warn when multi-phase design lacks plan hydration (from architect orch-go-865v3)
Fix Ctrl+D triple-bind: rebind session_delete to non-conflicting key
description: In Dylan's opencode fork, Ctrl+D is bound to THREE actions: app_exit (config.ts:771), session_delete (config.ts:784), and input_delete (config.ts:878). Muscle memory for 'exit' triggers deletion when session list dialog is focused. Confirmation is just red-highlighted title text, easy to miss in fast interaction. Fix: rebind session_delete from ctrl+d to a different key in opencode/src/config/config.ts:784. See model: .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #3)

title: Extract runDaemonLoop into composable functions in cmd/orch/daemon.go: (1) daemonSetup() for PID lock + config + wiring, (2) logDaemonConfig() for startup logging, (3) processDaemonCompletions() for completion loop + event logging, (4) runDaemonSpawnCycle() for inner spawn loop. Knowledge-tree Work tab: hide or translate triage labels into user-meaningful status
description:
The Work tab shows raw 'triage:ready' and 'triage:review' labels on most issues. These are internal daemon workflow labels (controls whether daemon auto-spawns or orchestrator reviews first) and are meaningless to the user.

Options:
1. **Hide triage: labels entirely** — they're operational plumbing, not user-facing
2. **Translate** — triage:ready → 'queued', triage:review → 'needs review' (if status is useful)
3. **Filter to meaningful labels only** — show area:, effort:, priority but suppress workflow labels

Recommendation: option 1 (hide). The triage state is internal orchestration machinery. User cares about area, priority, and status — not daemon routing.

title: Knowledge-tree: intelligent sort instead of alphabetical
description:
Currently the Knowledge tab sorts clusters (folders) alphabetically, and nodes within clusters are also alphabetical. This is the least useful ordering — tells you nothing about importance, recency, or connectivity.

Better sort options:
- **Recency** (last modified) — recently touched knowledge floats to top. Probably best default.
- **Connectivity** (most links/references to other nodes) — central/hub knowledge ranks higher
- **Staleness** (oldest without updates) — surfaces decaying knowledge
- **Activity heat** (recent probe/investigation count) — shows active learning areas

Recommendation: default to recency sort, with a sort toggle allowing user to switch between recency/connectivity/alphabetical. Staleness could be a visual indicator (dimming) rather than a separate sort.

title: Calibrate health score formula — separate test file threshold (2000 vs 800), scale saturation thresholds to codebase size (max(20, 10% source files) for bloated, max(15, 5% source files) for hotspots). Currently 24/50 bloated files are tests, zeroing two score dimensions unfairly. See .kb/plans/2026-03-10-harness-health-improvement.md Phase 1.

title: Surface verification tracker status in daemon bar and add gate banner
description:
## Context

The daemon's VerificationTracker pauses spawning when unverified completions exceed threshold (default 3). This state is only visible when a spawn fails in the terminal. The dashboard should show:
1. Current verification counter (e.g., '2/3 unverified')
2. Whether daemon is paused due to verification gate
3. Action hint ('Run orch complete to resume')

## Data source

Daemon status file (~/.orch/daemon-status.json) already has verification field:
```json
{
  "verification": {
    "is_paused": true,
    "completions_since_verification": 3,
    "threshold": 3,
    "remaining_before_pause": 0,
    "last_verification": "2026-02-15T..."
  }
}
```

But handleDaemon() in serve_system.go doesn't include this in the API response.

## Task

1. Add verification fields to DaemonAPIResponse in serve_system.go
2. Populate from status.Verification in handleDaemon()
3. Add verification fields to DaemonStatus interface in daemon.ts store
4. Display in daemon status bar: 'Verification: 2/3' or 'PAUSED (3/3)'
5. When paused, show amber/red banner below header (like focus breadcrumb)

## Files to modify
- cmd/orch/serve_system.go - add verification to DaemonAPIResponse + handleDaemon
- web/src/lib/stores/daemon.ts - add verification fields to DaemonStatus
- web/src/routes/work-graph/+page.svelte - add verification display in daemon bar + banner

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] /api/daemon response includes verification object when enabled
- [ ] Daemon bar shows verification counter
- [ ] Amber banner appears when daemon is paused for verification
- [ ] Banner includes action hint text
- [ ] go build ./cmd/orch/ succeeds

Set session TTL at spawn time to enable automatic cleanup
description:
OpenCode session TTL is implemented in the fork (Feb 14) but orch-go never sets it at spawn time. Sessions accumulate indefinitely, requiring manual orch clean --sessions.

FIX: In pkg/spawn/backends/headless.go, add time_ttl to the CreateSession call. Worker sessions should get a TTL (e.g., 4 hours after last update). Orchestrator sessions should get no TTL (persist until manually cleaned).

EXISTING CODE:
- headless.go:95-100 already passes metadata to CreateSession
- OpenCode fork supports time_ttl field on session creation (commit f3c3865b8)
- Periodic cleanup job in OpenCode runs every 5 minutes, deletes expired sessions
- Respects active prompts — wont delete sessions with status = "busy"

SCOPE:
1. Add time_ttl to CreateSessionRequest in pkg/opencode/client.go
2. Set TTL at spawn time in headless.go (e.g., 4h for workers, none for orchestrators)
3. Verify OpenCode cleanup job picks up expired sessions

VERIFICATION:
- Spawned worker sessions have time_ttl set
- Sessions auto-expire after TTL (check OpenCode DB)
- Orchestrator sessions persist (no TTL)
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

bd sync fails when concurrent agents leave unstaged changes in shared working tree
description:
bd sync does git pull --rebase which fails when other concurrent agents have uncommitted changes in the shared working tree. The agent's beads changes are ready to export but the pull step blocks everything.

FIX OPTIONS (in order of preference):
1. Use git pull --autostash — git handles stash/unstash automatically around the rebase. Built-in, handles most cases.
2. Skip pull when working tree is dirty — export and commit .beads/ changes, defer pull to next clean sync. Agent gets its export done, pull happens later.
3. Manual stash — git stash before pull, git stash pop after. Works but stash conflicts possible.

The key insight: agents care about EXPORTING their beads changes (commit + push). The pull step is a nice-to-have for staying current but shouldn't block the export.

title: Completion flow: add explain-back verification step to orch complete
description: Current orch complete checks agent self-reported success (Phase:Complete, tests pass, deliverables exist). Missing: human comprehension verification. New step in orch complete: after showing synthesis/results, system asks Dylan to explain what was built and why in his own words. His explanation is captured as a beads comment or artifact. This creates: (1) An unfakeable verification gate - cant rubber-stamp a conversational explanation. (2) A comprehension record for future sessions - next Claude can read Dylans understanding. (3) The distinction between agent-done and human-verified. Design: Could be conversational (orchestrator asks, Dylan answers) or structured (fill in: what it does, why it matters, how you verified). The explain-back IS the gate - completion doesnt close until its provided. Analogy: pilot callouts in instrument flying - forces processing, not just glancing.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION OUTCOME (Feb 14):

Two-gate verification model:
1. COMPREHENSION GATE (what/so-what):
   - Orchestrator presents 'the what' - factual scaffolding of what was built
   - Dylan explains 'the so-what' - what it means for his workflow, what worries him
   - Design principles: safe to be wrong, not starting from nothing, not given the answer
   - The split: AI provides the what, human provides the so-what

2. BEHAVIORAL GATE (tiered by deliverable type):
   - Feature: run the command, see the result
   - Bug fix: reproduce the bug, confirm it is gone
   - Refactor: run tests, basic smoke test
   - Investigation/decision: no behavioral gate needed - artifact IS the deliverable

Order: comprehension first, then behavioral. No point watching something work if you dont understand what its supposed to do.

Key design constraint from Dylan: system cant do the thinking for him. Leading questions that Dylan just confirms = rubber-stamping with extra steps. The so-what must be genuinely Dylans articulation.

Tested live in session: used orch-go-66j (skill-class awareness gate fix) as test case. Dylan confirmed the interaction felt right - made him think but not too much.

title: Design: system-prompt skill injection for spawn
description:
DISCOVERY: Claude CLI supports --system-prompt and --append-system-prompt in interactive mode (not just --print). Currently skills load via ~/.claude/skills/ auto-discovery → injected as user-level context, competing with SPAWN_CONTEXT.md, CLAUDE.md, and everything else for attention. The behavioral grammars model (Feb 24 probe) found system prompt has structural priority over user-level content. The dilution curve (~4 behavioral constraint budget) was measured with skill content at user level — the effective budget at system level could be significantly higher.

OPPORTUNITY: orch spawn could pass --append-system-prompt {skill_content} to claude CLI, injecting skills at system prompt level instead of user-level auto-discovery. This would give skill content structural priority in the attention hierarchy.

QUESTIONS FOR ARCHITECT:
1. --append-system-prompt vs --system-prompt? Append preserves Claude Code base instructions. Replace gives full control but loses base behavior.
2. Should SPAWN_CONTEXT.md remain user-level while skill content moves to system level? Or should both move?
3. Does --disable-slash-commands become the default for spawns (prevent double-loading from both system-prompt injection and auto-discovery)?
4. What happens to the skillc test harness? It already uses --system-prompt — does this make test conditions match production conditions (good) or lose the ability to test user-level vs system-level injection (tradeoff)?
5. Does this change the dilution curve? The ~4 behavioral constraint budget was measured at user level. An architect should design an experiment to measure the budget at system level.

EVIDENCE:
- claude --help shows --system-prompt and --append-system-prompt without '(only works with --print)' restriction
- skillc test already uses --system-prompt successfully in --print mode
- Behavioral grammars model: .kb/global/models/behavioral-grammars/model.md (Claims 1-3 on dilution and attention)
- Feb 24 probe: system prompt has structural priority over user-level content
- Dilution threshold probe: ~4 behavioral constraint budget at user level
- Spawn code: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go, pkg/spawn/ — current skill injection path

Review queue visibility: dashboard + orchestrator awareness
description:
Two visibility gaps discovered during verifiability-first bootstrap:

1. DASHBOARD: No review queue view. The dashboard shows issues by status (triage, in_progress) but has no first-class view of 'completed agents waiting for two-gate review.' Dylan had no visibility into the fact that 4 completions were waiting — had to run orch review in CLI. The review queue is the primary interface for verifiability-first: it's where Dylan does Gate 1 (comprehension) and Gate 2 (behavioral). It should be the most prominent thing in the dashboard, not invisible.

2. ORCHESTRATOR AWARENESS: The AI orchestrator should have the review queue as persistent context — like the HUD concept in orch-go-s0eg but specifically for 'N completions awaiting your review.' The orchestrator and Dylan work through the queue together (orchestrator presents the-what, Dylan explains the-so-what). The orchestrator needs to know the queue state without running orch review each time.

The orch tree CLI already has work view infrastructure (orch tree --work). The dashboard has a Knowledge Tree with work view. The data exists — daemon:ready-review label marks completed work. What's missing is surfacing it as a primary workflow, not buried in a tree of 21 issues.

Prior art: orch-go-6rw (daemon rework to ready-for-review model), orch-go-s0eg (HUD concept), orch tree CLI (work view with status).

Success criteria: Dylan opens dashboard and immediately sees 'You have N completions to review' with enough context to start Gate 1 without running CLI commands.

description: Phase 2 of verifiability-first decision. Create ~/.orch/control-heartbeat file written by Dylan (not agents) when verification happens. Contains timestamp of last human verification activity. Daemon reads this before spawning — stale heartbeat (>24h) halts autonomous spawning. This is the primary defense against multi-day autonomous drift (the 26-day third entropy spiral had zero human commits). Need: 1) Heartbeat file schema and location (~/.orch/control-heartbeat). 2) orch verify heartbeat command to update timestamp. 3) Automatic heartbeat update when orch complete --explain runs. 4) Daemon checks heartbeat age before each spawn cycle — stale = halt with clear error message. title: Add 'orch account configure' command for setting tier/role/config_dir
description:
## Problem
The only way to set account metadata (tier, role, config_dir) is manual YAML editing. This is fragile and the metadata gets lost (see: recurring metadata loss bug).

## Solution
Add `orch account configure <name>` command:
```
orch account configure work --tier 20x --role spillover --config-dir ~/.claude
orch account configure personal --role primary
```

Does a targeted load-modify-save that only updates specified fields.

## Acceptance
- `orch account configure <name> --tier/--role/--config-dir` works
- Only specified fields are modified
- `orch account list` shows updated values

Design session: structural solutions to accretion gravity in agentic coding
description:
## Problem (Systemic)

Accretion gravity is a fundamental constraint of task-scoped agents: agents add code where similar code exists because that's their only signal about where code belongs. No agent will spontaneously create a new package — that's not in their task.

The prior architect session (orch-go-4mu) designed 4 detection/warning layers (CLAUDE.md, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching). But detection without direction creates stuck agents. The principle says "structural constraints, not better agents" — and all 4 layers try to make better-informed agents.

## The Gap

None of the proposed solutions tell agents WHERE to put code. They only say "don't add here." An agent told "don't add to spawn_cmd.go" still doesn't know that pkg/spawn/gates/hotspot.go is the right target.

## Questions to Explore

1. **Why do agents accrete?** Because file proximity is their only architectural signal. What other signals could exist?
2. **What would a structural fix look like?** Not detection — actual constraints that make accretion physically harder than the alternative.
3. **Is extraction-first viable?** Before adding features to bloated files, could we spawn extraction first to create landing zones?
4. **Can spawn context carry architectural intent?** Orchestrator decides "this code goes in pkg/X/Y.go" at spawn time — agent doesn't choose.
5. **What do other agentic coding systems do?** Is this a solved problem anywhere?
6. **Does package structure itself prevent accretion?** If pkg/spawn/gates/ exists with clear purpose, do agents naturally use it?

## What This Is NOT

- NOT another detection/warning system
- NOT CLAUDE.md rules agents will ignore under pressure
- NOT gates that fire after the damage is done

## Constraint

This is a design session for Dylan + orchestrator, not a spawnable investigation. The solution needs to be general enough to apply to any agentic coding system, not just orch-go.
notes:
## Design Session Outcome (Feb 14 2026)

Rejected architect's 4-layer detection/warning approach. Detection without direction creates stuck agents.

**Actual solution: Invert the gravity field.**

1. Extract concerns from monolithic files into focused packages (strangler fig)
2. Agents naturally discover and route to focused packages via search (proven by probe orch-go-8ao)
3. Monolithic files shrink as new work routes to attractors AND agents pull existing code into them

**Probe evidence:** Agent given "add rate-limit gate to spawn" with NO mention of pkg/spawn/gates/ — created ratelimit.go there anyway AND extracted 173 lines from spawn_cmd.go.

**Who extracts:** Two tracks:
1. Orchestrator judgment at triage time (manual, current)
2. Daemon auto-extraction when targeting CRITICAL hotspots (automated, orch-go-TBD)

**Key insight:** Agents don't need architectural docs or rules. They need directory structure that makes the right choice obvious from search results. The file system IS the architecture.

title: Design artifact sync mechanism — skills, CLAUDE.md, and models drift as features land, soft conventions haven't held
description:
Recurring coordination failure: agents ship features but don't update the artifacts that reference those features (orchestrator skill, CLAUDE.md, harness-engineering model, etc). Dylan flags this periodically. Prior soft fixes (reminders in skills, completion checklist items) haven't stuck — consistent with harness model prediction that convention without gate erodes.

Two design directions to evaluate:
1. Completion gate: detect that agent modified files covered by a skill/model, block until referencing artifact is updated. Risk: high FP rate, most feature-impl agents shouldn't edit skills.
2. Periodic sync agent: OpenAI's 'garbage collection' pattern — background agent reviews recent commits against artifact inventory, produces update PRs. Decouples feature work from artifact maintenance.

Key question: is the update responsibility per-agent (gate) or periodic (sync agent)? Per-agent is harder but immediate. Periodic is easier but introduces lag.

Evidence: this session alone shipped --explore flag, orch review synthesize, exploration-judge skill, gate signal-vs-noise fixes — none updated the orchestrator skill or CLAUDE.md. The drift is real-time.

title: Rework explain-back gate: conversational via orchestrator, not CLI prompts
description:
The current explain-back implementation (pkg/verify/explain_back.go) uses interactive CLI prompts - three fixed questions that Dylan types answers to in a terminal. This doesnt match how Dylan actually works: he works through the AI orchestrator conversationally.

CURRENT (wrong model):
  Dylan runs orch complete → CLI prompts 'What did you build?' → Dylan types answer → repeat x3

CORRECT (matches actual workflow):
  Orchestrator presents 'the what' → Dylan explains 'the so-what' conversationally → orchestrator captures explanation → orchestrator runs orch complete --explain 'summary'

CHANGES NEEDED:
1. Remove interactive PromptExplainBack() stdin/stdout prompting from pkg/verify/explain_back.go
2. Change --skip-explain-back flag to --explain 'text' flag on orch complete
3. Gate on --explain being non-empty when explain-back gate is active
4. Store the explanation as beads comment (this part already works)
5. Keep GateExplainBack constant and skip logic for orchestrator sessions / untracked agents

The conversational quality check (is Dylans explanation sufficient?) stays with the AI orchestrator, not the CLI. The CLIs job is: accept explanation, store it, gate on non-empty.

WHY: Fixed CLI questions can be answered mechanically (rubber-stamping). Conversational verification cant - the orchestrator can follow up, challenge, ask 'what worries you about it?' This was validated in the design session where we verified 4 deliverables conversationally and it worked well.

CONSTRAINT: AI sycophancy undermines explain-back verification (kb-afa611). The orchestrator must not mirror Dylans answers back or validate incomplete understanding.

Prior commit: 61ee67c3 (feat: add explain-back verification gate to orch complete)
Files to modify: pkg/verify/explain_back.go, cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go

title: orch spawn should explicitly set CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker for spawned agents
description: Hardening: orch spawn should explicitly pass CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker when launching agents via Claude CLI. Currently workers inherit whatever CLAUDE_CONTEXT is in the environment, which caused contamination when CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator was set globally in settings.json. Even though that was reverted, explicit is better than implicit — workers should always declare their context so hooks (gate-orchestrator-code-access.py, gate-orchestrator-task-tool.py) reliably skip them. Implementation: in pkg/spawn/claude.go, add CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker to the env vars passed to the claude CLI invocation.

title: Spawn context kb relevance: query matches skill names instead of domain topics, drowning task-specific FRAME
description:
PROBLEM: orch spawn assembles spawn context by running kb context with the task title as query. For 'Architect: Redesign pricing comparison KPIs', the query matched on 'architect' and 'redesign' as keywords, pulling 22 entries (66KB) of orch-go infrastructure knowledge (gate calibration, toolchain failures, coaching plugin probes). Zero entries about toolshed pricing or KPI design.

TWO ROOT CAUSES:
1. Query relevance — kb context matches on skill/concept names (architect, redesign) rather than domain topics (pricing, KPI). Global/orch-go entries dominate over project-specific entries.
2. FRAME not in spawn context — the beads issue FRAME comment (containing the actual strategic insight) is not assembled into SPAWN_CONTEXT.md. Agent must manually bd show to find it.

EXPECTED: Spawn context should (1) include FRAME from beads comments front and center, (2) prefer project-specific kb entries over global ones, (3) filter by domain relevance not keyword overlap with skill names.

description: Problem: When orch complete runs for agent A, sibling agents B and C (running in the same workers-orch-go tmux session) die. Their tmux windows disappear and they show as phantom in orch status. This has happened repeatedly this session — every time we complete an agent, other in-flight agents die. The t95 fix (overmind restart api instead of kill) landed but agents still die. Evidence from this session: (1) orch-go-9 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (2) orch-go-7 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (3) Earlier: pw-8966 and orch-go-1 died the same way. Timeline: agents spawned ~23:30, orch-go-8 completed at ~23:56, orch-go-9 and orch-go-7 went phantom immediately after. Possible causes: (1) make install replaces the orch binary mid-execution for active agents. (2) overmind restart api has side effects on tmux. (3) Claude CLI sessions crash when OpenCode server restarts. (4) Some other orch complete side effect kills sibling windows.

Headless spawn doesn't set agent working directory to --workdir/cwd
description: Problem: When spawning headless agents for cross-project work (e.g., from orch-go orchestrator targeting price-watch), the agent's shell starts in orch-go (likely the daemon's cwd or a hardcoded default) instead of the target project directory. PROJECT_DIR is included in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md as prompt text, but this doesn't control the actual working directory of the spawned session. The agent has to manually cd, and bd commands fail before it does (e.g., 'bd comment pw-w7z0' fails because it's looking in orch-go's .beads/ which has no pw- prefix issues). Evidence: Agent pw-feat-build-scsoauthclient-service-13feb-7c9d started in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go instead of /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch. SPAWN_CONTEXT.md had correct PROJECT_DIR but shell cwd was wrong. Agent had to cd manually and bd comment failed before it did. Impact: P1 because this affects ALL cross-project headless spawns. Agents waste context discovering wrong directory, bd phase reporting fails, and early commands operate on wrong project's beads DB. Fix: The headless spawn backend (OpenCode HTTP API) needs to set the session's working directory to the --workdir flag value or the orchestrator's cwd at spawn time.

title: Account re-auth flow drops metadata (tier/role/config_dir)
description:
## Problem
When a token refresh fails and user follows the recommended re-auth flow (`orch account remove work && orch account add work`), all account metadata (tier, role, config_dir) is lost. AddAccount in oauth.go:311 creates a bare Account struct with only Email, RefreshToken, Source.

This is a recurring bug — metadata has been manually restored multiple times.

## Root Cause
Two issues:
1. `AddAccount()` creates a bare `Account{}` struct without merging existing metadata
2. The recommended re-auth guidance in `TokenRefreshError.ActionableGuidance()` tells users to remove+add, which destroys metadata by design

## Fix Options
**Option A (merge on add):** In `AddAccount()`, check if account exists and preserve Tier/Role/ConfigDir from existing entry before overwriting  
**Option B (preserve on remove):** Store metadata separately so it survives remove+add cycles  
**Option A is simpler and sufficient.**

Also: consider adding race protection (file locking) to SaveConfig since daemon, dashboard, and CLI all do concurrent load-modify-save cycles on accounts.yaml.

## Acceptance
- Re-auth flow preserves tier/role/config_dir
- Test: add account with metadata, remove+add, verify metadata survives
- Bonus: concurrent SaveConfig calls don't clobber each other

title: Account routing uses weekly usage instead of 5-hour window, ignores tier
description: Account routing heuristic needs two fixes: (1) Route based on 5-hour window headroom, not weekly — the 5-hour window is what actually blocks agents. (2) Factor in tier when comparing headroom — 20x at 86% weekly has far more absolute remaining capacity than 5x at 57%. The routing should pick the account with the most absolute 5-hour headroom (percentage remaining * tier multiplier).

Wire VerificationTracker into daemon run loop (dead code from orch-go-7jl)
description:
VerificationTracker was implemented by orch-go-7jl (commit 85a0e021) with working tests. But it is NEVER CALLED in the daemon's actual run loop.

Evidence:
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go lines 201-204: VerificationTracker field exists on Daemon struct
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go lines 235, 262: NewVerificationTracker() called in constructors
- grep for RecordCompletion/IsPaused/RecordHumanVerification in daemon.go and completion.go: ZERO results

The tracker has 3 methods that must be wired:
1. RecordCompletion() - must be called after each daemon auto-completion
2. IsPaused() - must be checked before daemon spawns new agents
3. RecordHumanVerification() - must be called when orch complete runs (manual human verification)

Without this wiring, the daemon spawns indefinitely without human verification — the exact failure mode the verifiability-first decision was created to prevent (26-day spiral, 1163 commits, zero human involvement).

Also need: orch daemon resume command to call RecordHumanVerification when Dylan is ready to release more work.

Related: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Constraint 3: Mechanical Enforcement)
